qas.q: Describe the appearance of the normal chest on an X-ray
qas.a:
- both hemidiaphragms are Round (dome-shaped)
- the right hemidiaphragm is slightly higher than the left
- the right hemidiaphragm is at the level of the sixth anterior rib
- Trachea is midline, bilateral radiolucent appearance of lungs, with sharp costophrenic angles.

qas.q:
Describe the following terms in relation to the quality of a radiologic image

Exposure/Penetration
qas.a:
Will show the intervertebral disc spaces thru the shadow of the mediastinum
(vertebrae are just visible behind the heart)

qas.q:
Describe the following terms in relation to the quality of a radiologic image

Under- penetration
qas.a:
Image does not allow visualization of the intervertebral discs thru the heart shadow.
(all kinds of white)

qas.q:
Describe the following terms in relation to the quality of a radiologic image

Over- penetration
qas.a: Image will show black lung parenchyma without blood vessels.

qas.q: What condition causes obliteration of the costophrenic angles ?
qas.a: Pleura Effusion

qas.q: In what pathology is the diaphragm flattened ?
qas.a: COPD

qas.q: What pathology would cause crowding of the ribs ?
qas.a:
Atelectasis
(straight or horizontal ribs = Air trapping)

qas.q:
Describe each of the following positions used for chest X-rays and what pathology each can identify.

Lateral decubitus position
qas.a:
Patient lying on the affected side

Detects
small pleural effusion

qas.q:
Describe each of the following positions used for chest X-rays and what pathology each can identify.

End expiratory image
qas.a:
Taken when the patient is at the end exhalation

Detects
-Small pneumothorax
-diaphragmatic excursion

qas.q: Where should the tip of the endotracheal tube be positioned when viewed on a chest roentgenogram ?
qas.a:
-Below the vocal cords
- Approximately 2 - 6 cm above the carina
- At the level of the aortic knob or aortic arch

qas.q: What is the quickest way to determine adequate ventilation following endotracheal intubation ?
qas.a: - Inspection and auscultation

qas.q: Proper position for Chest tube
qas.a: Should be located in the pleural space surrounding the lung.

qas.q: Proper position for Nasogastric and Feeding tube
qas.a: Should be positioned in the stomach 2 - 6 cm below the diaphragm

qas.q: Proper position for Pulmonary artery catheter
qas.a: Should appear in the right lower lung field.

qas.q: Proper position for Pacemaker
qas.a: should be normally positioned in the right ventricle

qas.q: Proper position Central venous catheter
qas.a:
Inserted through the subclavian or jugular vein and should rest in the superior vena cava or right atrium of the heart
(4th intercostal space, right of sternum)

qas.q:
Briefly describe how each of the following conditions would be represented on a lateral neck X-ray

Croup
qas.a:
Narrowing with subglottic swelling
-Steeple sign
-Picket fence sign
-Pencil point sign
-Hourglass sign

qas.q:
Briefly describe how each of the following conditions would be represented on a lateral neck X-ray

Epiglottitis
qas.a:
Supraglottic narrowing with an enlarged and flattened epiglottis and swollen aryepiglottic folds
-Thumb sign

qas.q: What type of radiographs are helpful in locating areas of air trapping?
qas.a: Inspiratory and Expiratory radiographs

qas.q:
Radiolucent
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Dark pattern, air

Normal

qas.q:
Radiodense/ Opacity
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
White pattern, solid, fluid

Normal for bone, organs

qas.q:
Infiltrate
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Any ill-defined radiodensity

Atelectasis

qas.q:
Consolidation
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Solid white area

Pneumonia/ Pleural effusion

qas.q:
Hyperlucency
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Extra pulmonary air
(Air trapping)

COPD, Asthma attack, Pneumothorax

qas.q:
Vascular markings
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Lymphatics, vessels, lung tissue

Increased with CHF absent with Pneumothorax

qas.q:
Diffuse
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a:
Spread throughout

Atelectasis/ Pneumonia

qas.q:
Opaque
a) Description
b) Diagnosis
qas.a: Fluid, solid

qas.q:
Pulmonary Edema
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) Fluffy infiltrates, butterfly pattern, batwing pattern

b) Diffuse whiteness, infiltrate in shape of butterfly

c) Diuretics, Digitalis, Digoxin

qas.q:
Atelectasis
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) Patchy infiltrates, platelike infiltrates, crowded pulmonary vessels, crowded air bronchograms

b) Scattered densities, thin layered densities

c) Lung Expansion Therapy, SMI/IS, IPPB, CPAP, PEEP

qas.q:
ARDS or IRDS
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) Ground glass appearance, honeycomb pattern, diffuse bilateral radiopacity

b) Reticulogranular (glass), Reticulonodular

c) O2 (recruit) Low VT or PIP (alveoli) , CPAP, PEEP

qas.q:
Pleural effusion
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) Blunting/ obliteration of the costophrenic angle, Basilar infiltrates with meniscus (curving) Concave superior interface/ border

b) Fluid level on affected side, possible mediastinal shift to unaffected side

c) Thoracentesis , Chest tube , antibiotics, steroids

qas.q:
Pneumonia
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) air bronchogram

b) increased density from consolidation and atelectasis

c) antibiotics

qas.q:
Pulmonary Embolism
a) Terminology
b) Description
c) Treatment
qas.a:
a) Peripheral wedge- shape

b) may be normal

c) heparin, Streptokinase

qas.q: Tuberculosis
qas.a:
a) Cavity formation

b) Often in upper lobe

c) Antitubercular agents
